---
title: "Model-driven experimental design: resolving DTS Ca²⁺ depletion — and the whole-cell model as a hypothesis engine"
author: "Steve Haigh (with Claude Code)"
date: "2026-06-14"
format:
  pdf:
    include-in-header: ../pandoc-header.tex
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
---

# Purpose

A mechanistic whole-cell model earns its scientific keep not by *fitting* existing
data but by making **falsifiable predictions in regimes nobody has measured**, and
by telling an experimentalist **what to measure, why it discriminates competing
hypotheses, and which perturbations carry the most information.** This memo works
that idea through on a concrete open question raised by the recovery-phase
analysis — *does the platelet dense tubular system (DTS) Ca²⁺ store deplete
completely or only partially under sustained agonist?* — and uses it to sketch a
wet-lab project the model could drive.

Companion document: `dts-depletion-literature-2026-06-14.qmd` (the literature
check that frames the question and shows it is genuinely unmeasured).

# The open question (recap)

Under the default saturating agonist our model drains free [Ca²⁺]_DTS to ~0 µM;
Dolan & Diamond (2014) retain ~120–180 µM. The literature is split and, crucially,
the discriminating quantity — **free luminal [Ca²⁺] in the platelet DTS during and
after stimulation** — has never been directly measured (platelets are tiny and
anucleate, so the field reports Ca *content* / releasable pools, not free luminal
concentration). The proteome (Burkhart 2012) does not settle it: the IP₃R:SERCA
*abundance* ratio actually favours *deeper* depletion, so the residual-store
behaviour seen in other cells must be a **kinetic (IP₃R-inactivation) property** —
exactly the kind of thing a parts-list cannot resolve and an experiment must.

# Why it's unmeasured — the measurement problem

Directly reading free [Ca²⁺]_DTS needs a **low-affinity** Ca²⁺ reporter (store
[Ca²⁺] is ~10²–10³ µM, far above cytosolic indicators' range) that is **localised
to the store** and **read in intact, stimulated cells**. Three routes:

1. **ER-targeted genetically-encoded indicator (gold standard).** A low-affinity
   ER GECI — e.g. R-CEPIA1er / GEM-CEPIA1er, or a D4ER cameleon (K_d in the
   hundreds-of-µM range). Platelets cannot be transfected, so the sensor is
   expressed in the **lineage**: a megakaryocyte/platelet-lineage transgenic
   (e.g. PF4-Cre × floxed ER-GECI) or **iPSC-derived megakaryocytes**, then the
   shed platelets/proplatelets are imaged. Gives real-time free [Ca²⁺]_DTS during
   agonist stimulation — the depletion nadir *and* whether/when it refills.
2. **Low-affinity chemical indicator** (Mag-Fluo-4 / Mag-Fura-2) loaded, then the
   plasma membrane selectively permeabilised (digitonin) to strip cytosolic dye,
   leaving store-trapped dye. Reports the IP₃-releasable fraction in permeabilised
   cells — good for the *quantal* question ("what % does maximal IP₃ release?") but
   loses the intact sustained-receptor context.
3. **Releasable-pool / content** assay (stimulate, then dump the residual with
   ionomycin/thapsigargin and quantify the remaining releasable Ca²⁺). The most
   platelet-tractable with standard tools, but it measures *content*, not free
   concentration.

# The discriminating experiment — what the model predicts

The model does not merely predict "complete vs partial." It predicts a
**regime-dependent surface**, and *that surface is the experimental design*:

- **P1 — depth scales with dose.** The store minimum deepens monotonically from
  partial (threshold agonist) to near-complete (saturating). → Measure
  [Ca²⁺]_DTS minimum across an **agonist dose-response**.
- **P2 — refill is dose-dependent, and it is the discriminator.** Below threshold
  the store *refills* in the recovery phase; under saturating sustained agonist it
  stays empty (sustained IP₃ holds the IP₃R open). → Measure the **time course**,
  not just the nadir. Partial-depletion models predict refill at all doses; ours
  predicts refill **only below threshold**. This is the cleanest falsification.
- **P3 — the cause is IP₃ persistence, not abundance.** Forcing IP₃ to persist
  (a non-hydrolysable IP₃ analogue, or IP₃-5-phosphatase inhibition) should
  *prevent* refill even at low dose; removing the autocrine amplifiers
  (aspirin + ecto-NTPDase to clear secreted ADP) should *restore* refill at
  intermediate dose. → A mechanistic perturbation arm.
- **P4 — an IP₃R-inactivation perturbation moves depletion; a SERCA copy-number
  change barely does.** Because the depth is conductance/gating-limited, the model
  ranks IP₃R-inactivation modulation as high-information and SERCA abundance as
  low-information for this observable.

These four are quantitative, pre-registerable predictions a collaborator can
confirm or kill.

# How the model drives the design

1. **It identifies the critical measurement** — free [Ca²⁺]_DTS depletion/refill
   dynamics — and *why* it matters (it discriminates IP₃R-inactivation hypotheses
   that a proteome and the existing 30-s peak data cannot).
2. **It makes the predictions falsifiable and quantitative** (P1–P4), so the
   experiments are hypothesis-driven rather than exploratory.
3. **It prioritises perturbations by sensitivity.** In-silico sweeps rank which
   knockouts move the observable most (IP₃R inactivation ≫ SERCA abundance), so
   bench effort goes to the highest-information experiments. The **STIM1 8×
   abundance discrepancy** (Burkhart ~33,000 vs the model's Dolan-sourced ~4,265)
   is itself a high-value, testable SOCE-side prediction.
4. **It is the inference framework for the readout.** Fit the recalibrated model
   (IP₃R inactivation; STIM1 count) to the new store-Ca data, then test whether it
   *independently* predicts the integrin PAC-1 and granule-secretion
   dose-responses — a genuine model → experiment → model loop, not a one-shot fit.
5. **It spins out secondary hypotheses** the same machinery can test: is the
   second wave abolished by aspirin/NTPDase at matched store depletion? does the
   PAC-1 dose-response show the predicted switch-like threshold?

# A project this model could drive (sketch)

> **"Dynamics of dense-tubular-system Ca²⁺ depletion and refilling in human
> platelets, and their control by IP₃R inactivation and autocrine amplification."**
>
> - **Aim 1 — measure it.** Free [Ca²⁺]_DTS across agonist dose and time, via an
>   ER-targeted low-affinity GECI in lineage-transgenic / iPSC-MK platelets
>   (the direct measurement the field lacks). Outputs the depth- and
>   refill-vs-dose curves (tests P1, P2).
> - **Aim 2 — test the mechanism.** Sustain-IP₃ and remove-autocrine perturbations
>   on refill; an IP₃R-inactivation perturbation on depth (tests P3, P4).
> - **Aim 3 — close the loop.** Recalibrate the model to the store-Ca data, then
>   validate that it predicts the PAC-1 and secretion dose-responses on
>   independent readouts.

This is a fundable, PhD-scale arc in which the model is the engine at every
stage: it finds the gap, predicts the discriminating observables, prioritises the
perturbations, and provides the inference framework.

# Feasibility caveats (stated honestly)

- **Anucleate cells are the catch.** The GECI route requires megakaryocytes /
  transgenics / iPSC-MKs, and MK ER is a *proxy* for the platelet DTS (larger, not
  identical) — a real interpretive caveat.
- **SNR on a thin reticulum** in a 2–3 µm cell is hard; MK imaging or single-
  platelet TIRF with careful controls may be needed.
- **Single-cell model, some population readouts.** Aggregation is out of reach;
  store-Ca, PAC-1, and secretion are all per-cell and well-matched.

# The in-silico precursor: the dose-response study

The dry-lab half of Aim 1 is buildable **now**: sweep agonist dose in the model and
generate the predicted depletion-/refill-vs-dose curves plus the PAC-1 and
secretion dose-responses, with the autocrine-on/off contrast (the P2/P3
discriminator). That artefact is simultaneously (a) a model result and (b) the
**pre-registered prediction set** the wet-lab experiment would test. It is being
built as `runscripts/manual/runDoseResponse.py` (this commit series); its figures
are the concrete curves the experiment aims at (embedded below once generated).

*Render: `quarto render reports/design/model-driven-experiment-design-2026-06-14.qmd`.*
